name: Django
what_it_does: Batteries-included Python web framework providing an ORM, automatic admin interface, migrations system, authentication, and signal dispatch for building database-driven web applications.
use_cases: Scaffold a complete CRUD web application with database models, admin UI, and authentication in hours using Django's built-in components
Generate and apply database schema migrations automatically from model changes using makemigrations/migrate workflow
Build REST APIs quickly by combining Django with Django REST Framework (DRF) for serializers, viewsets, and permissions
Use Django signals to decouple application logic — trigger post-save actions, send notifications, or invalidate caches when model instances change
Leverage the Django ORM query API to construct complex database queries with filtering, annotation, and aggregation without raw SQL
not_for: High-throughput async microservices where Django's synchronous ORM and WSGI heritage create bottlenecks — use FastAPI or async frameworks instead
Tiny scripts or single-endpoint utilities where Django's setup overhead and conventions are excessive
Projects requiring a non-relational primary database — Django's ORM is built around relational databases and NoSQL support is limited
best_when: You're building a relational-database-backed web application in Python where developer velocity, built-in admin, and a mature ecosystem matter most.
avoid_when: You need native async-first performance, a micro-framework with minimal opinions, or your primary data store is non-relational.

auth.notes: Django is a library/framework installed via pip; no external auth required. Applications built with Django implement their own auth using Django's built-in authentication system.

pricing.notes: Free and open source; hosting costs depend on deployment platform choice.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Django's error pages and management command output are among the most developer-friendly in Python web frameworks; migration conflicts and ORM errors are clearly described.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Migrations are idempotent (already-applied migrations are skipped); ORM operations are not inherently idempotent — agents must use get_or_create / update_or_create patterns.
known_agent_gotchas: Migration dependency graph can become complex in large projects; agents auto-generating models must squash or linearize migrations to avoid merge conflicts
Django's ORM lazy evaluation means QuerySets are not executed until iterated — agents inspecting query results must force evaluation with list() or exists() to get actual data
The N+1 query problem is silent by default; agents generating model traversal code must use select_related() / prefetch_related() or risk severe performance degradation
SECRET_KEY and DEBUG settings must be correctly set for production — agents scaffolding deployment configs frequently leave DEBUG=True or hardcode SECRET_KEY
Django signals (post_save, pre_delete) execute synchronously in the request/response cycle; agents adding heavy signal handlers will block HTTP responses without explicit async handling

security_score_components.security_notes: Django has strong built-in security defaults (CSRF, XSS escaping, clickjacking protection, SQL injection prevention via ORM). TLS is a deployment concern. SECRET_KEY leakage is the most common agent-introduced vulnerability.
